title: The Morn That Breaks Its Heart Of Gold
content:
From an ode "In Commemoration of the Founding of the Massachusetts Bay Colony."

혻혻혻혻The morn that breaks its heart of gold
혻혻혻혻Above the purple hills;
혻혻혻혻The eve, that spills
혻혻혻혻Its nautilus splendor where the sea is rolled;
혻혻혻혻The night, that leads the vast procession in
혻혻혻혻Of stars and dreams, - 
혻혻혻혻The beauty that shall never die or pass: - 
혻혻혻혻The winds, that spin
혻혻혻혻Of rain the misty mantles of the grass,
혻혻혻혻And thunder raiment of the mountain-streams;
혻혻혻혻The sunbeams, penciling with gold the dusk
혻혻혻혻Green cowls of ancient woods;
혻혻혻혻The shadows, thridding, veiled with musk,
혻혻혻혻The moon-pathed solitudes,
혻혻혻혻Call to my Fancy, saying, "Follow! follow!"
혻혻혻혻Till, following, I see, - 
혻혻혻혻Fair as a cascade in a rainbowed hollow, - 
혻혻혻혻A dream, a shape, take form,
혻혻혻혻Clad on with every charm, - 

혻혻혻혻The vision of that Ideality,
혻혻혻혻Which lured the pioneer in wood and hill,
혻혻혻혻And beckoned him from earth and sky;
혻혻혻혻The dream that cannot die,
혻혻혻혻Their children's children did fulfill,
혻혻혻혻In stone and iron and wood,
혻혻혻혻Out of the solitude,
혻혻혻혻And by a stalwart act
혻혻혻혻Create a mighty fact - 
혻혻혻혻A Nation, now that stands
혻혻혻혻Clad on with hope and beauty, strength and song,
혻혻혻혻Eternal, young and strong,
혻혻혻혻Planting her heel on wrong,
혻혻혻혻Her starry banner in triumphant hands....

혻혻혻혻Within her face the rose
혻혻혻혻Of Alleghany dawns;
혻혻혻혻Limbed with Alaskan snows,
혻혻혻혻Floridian starlight in her eyes, - 
혻혻혻혻Eyes stern as steel yet tender as a fawn's, - 
혻혻혻혻And in her hair
혻혻혻혻The rapture of her rivers; and the dare,
혻혻혻혻As perishless as truth,
혻혻혻혻That o'er the crags of her Sierras flies,
혻혻혻혻Urging the eagle ardor through her veins,
혻혻혻혻Behold her where,
혻혻혻혻Around her radiant youth,

혻혻혻혻The spirits of the cataracts and plains,
혻혻혻혻The genii of the floods and forests, meet,
혻혻혻혻In rainbow mists circling her brow and feet:
혻혻혻혻The forces vast that sit
혻혻혻혻In session round her; powers paraclete,
혻혻혻혻That guard her presence; awful forms and fair,
혻혻혻혻Making secure her place;
혻혻혻혻Guiding her surely as the worlds through space
혻혻혻혻Do laws sidereal; edicts, thunder-lit,
혻혻혻혻Of skyed eternity, in splendor borne
혻혻혻혻On planetary wings of night and morn.

혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *

혻혻혻혻From her high place she sees
혻혻혻혻Her long procession of accomplished acts,
혻혻혻혻Cloud-winged refulgences
혻혻혻혻Of thoughts in steel and stone, of marble dreams,
혻혻혻혻Lift up tremendous battlements,
혻혻혻혻Sun-blinding, built of facts;
혻혻혻혻While in her soul she seems,
혻혻혻혻Listening, to hear, as from innumerable tents,
혻혻혻혻占퐋nian thunder, wonder, and applause
혻혻혻혻Of all the heroic ages that are gone;
혻혻혻혻Feeling secure
혻혻혻혻That, as her Past, her Future shall endure,
혻혻혻혻As did her Cause
혻혻혻혻When redly broke the dawn
혻혻혻혻Of fierce rebellion, and, beneath its star,
혻혻혻혻The firmaments of war
혻혻혻혻Poured down infernal rain,
혻혻혻혻And North and South lay bleeding mid their slain.
혻혻혻혻And now, no less, shall her great Cause prevail,
혻혻혻혻More so in peace than war,
혻혻혻혻Through the thrilled wire and electric rail,
혻혻혻혻Carrying her message far:
혻혻혻혻Shaping her dream
혻혻혻혻Within the brain of steam,
혻혻혻혻That, with a myriad hands,
혻혻혻혻Labors unceasingly, and knits her lands
혻혻혻혻In firmer union; joining plain and stream
혻혻혻혻With steel; and binding shore to shore
혻혻혻혻With bands of iron; - nerves and arteries,
혻혻혻혻Along whose adamant forever pour
혻혻혻혻Her concrete thoughts, her tireless energies.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Madison Julius Cawein delivers a powerful performance in "The Morn That Breaks Its Heart Of Gold"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
